title: Pouring a sheet: the Japanese nagashizuki method
subtitle: Forming handmade paper by pouring dilute pulp across the mould surface instead of dipping
excerpt: The nagashizuki method pours dilute pulp onto a flexible mould and rocks the frame to build up layers of fibre. Unlike the Western dip method, the mould is not submerged. The result is thinner, more uniform sheets suitable for calligraphy paper and book repair tissues.
sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Old Papermaking (1923), chapter on Japanese papermaking techniques including nagashizuki and tamezuki. Internet Archive: https://archive.org/details/oldpapermak00hunt. Hunter distinguishes the two primary Japanese sheet-forming methods and their characteristic paper qualities.

glossaryTerms.definition: The Japanese flowing-water method of sheet formation, in which dilute pulp is poured across a flexible mould and the frame is rocked to build up and drain the fibre layers. The excess pulp flows off the edge of the mould.

The Japanese still-water method, equivalent to the Western method of submerging the mould in a vat. The mould is dipped and lifted in a single motion; the fibre settles evenly as the water drains through the screen.

The removable frame that sits on top of the mould during sheet formation, defining the edges of the sheet. In nagashizuki, the deckle is often omitted; the sheet edge forms naturally as the pulp flows across the mould.

content.text: The two main Japanese methods for forming handmade paper produce distinctly different sheets.
follows the same approach as Western papermaking: dip the mould into a vat and lift it out.

replaces the vat with a ladle or jug: pulp is poured across the mould surface and the mould is rocked to build up layers, then tilted to drain the excess. The mould does not go into a vat at all. The result is a thinner, more translucent sheet with a characteristic rippled texture that comes from the rocking motion. Traditional washi for calligraphy, printmaking, and book repair is made this way.

You also need: a fine-mesh mould; a ladle or jug for pouring; a shallow tray to catch runoff; and felts for couching. Traditional nagashizuki moulds use a flexible bamboo or wire-screen grid. A Western mould with very fine screening works too. The pulp should be well-beaten kozo or cotton linter at roughly 0.5% consistency.

Traditional nagashizuki uses a mucilaginous plant extract called neri, made from tororo-aoi root, to slow drainage and extend the working time. At home scale, a few drops of liquid starch in the pulp has a similar, if less effective, result.
Hold the mould horizontally over the tray. Remove the
if using a Western mould: nagashizuki works without a deckle.

Pour the first scoop of dilute pulp onto the near edge of the mould. Tilt the mould away from you, allowing the pulp to flow across the surface as a thin film. Tilt back toward you to catch the runoff at the near edge.
Rock the mould gently side to side and front to back. The rocking motion distributes the fibres evenly before the water drains. The characteristic ripple of nagashizuki paper forms during this step.

Pour a second scoop of pulp onto the mould, rock again, and drain. Repeat until the sheet is thick enough. For a lightweight calligraphy paper, two or three pours is sufficient; for a heavier sheet, four to five.
Allow the mould to drain fully, then couch the sheet onto a felt as in the Western method.
Compared to the Western dip method

A Western sheet forms in one motion: the mould is dipped and lifted. The fibre settles by gravity through the water column as the mould drains. Nagashizuki builds the sheet in multiple thin layers, each rocked to distribute before the next pour. The result is a more interlocked fibre network, a thinner sheet for equivalent strength, and a characteristic undulating texture not found in Western paper.
